quotes.text: Vice, O king, is a mean thing, virtue is great and grand.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 4, ch. 8, sec. 31)

quotes.text: Let him do nothing mean.
I DEEM ... unrighteous actions contemptible.
He sustains his life by means that are quite pure.
Men going (to what they deserve) according to their deeds.
As men sow, thus shall they reap.
Reaping the fruit of right or evil doing, and sharing happiness or misery in consequence.
Your evil thoughts and evil words but hurt yourselves.
People grieve from selfishness.
The evil-doer suffers both in this world and in the next.

Hell was not created by any one. ... The fire of the angry mind produces the fire of hell, and consumes its possessor. When a person does evil, he lights the fire of hell, and burns with his own fire.
Doing good we reap good, just as a man who sows that which is sweet (enjoys the same).
He who does wrong, O king, comes to feel remorse. ... But he who does well feels no remorse, and feeling no remorse, gladness will spring up within him.
quotes.source: Questions of King Milinda (Book 3, ch. 7, sec. 7)

quotes.text: Morality brings happiness: ... at night one’s rest is peaceful, and on waking one is still happy.
If, then, you would please me, show pity to that poor wretch.
Oppressed with others' suffering.

A LOVING heart is the great requirement! to regard the people as an only son; not to oppress, not to destroy; ... not to exalt oneself by treading down others, but to comfort and befriend those in suffering.
He cares for and cherishes his people more than one would a naked and perishing child.
To give help to the impoverished, the orphan, and the aged.
The acts and the practice of religion, to wit, sympathy, charity, truthfulness, purity, gentleness, kindness.

Go ye, O Brethren, and wander forth, for the gain of the many, the welfare of the many, in compassion for the world, for the good, for the gain, for the welfare of ... men. ... Publish, O Brethren, the doctrine glorious. ... Preach ye a life of holiness ... perfect and pure.
Go, then, through every country, convert those not converted.
Go! therefore, each one traveling alone; filled with compassion, go! rescue and receive.
Is any man able for a moment to cause his neighbor to rise from a state of sin? The Buddha alone is able to save the world.
As a mother, even at the risk of her own life, protects her son, her only son: so let there be goodwill without measure among all beings.
No doctrine, whether it be of an enemy or of another without cause, but on the contrary every word of honor, is the word of Buddha.
Religion is nothing but the faculty of love.
Should those who are not with us, O Brethren, speak in dispraise of me, or of my doctrine, or of the church, that is no reason why you should give way to anger.
Why should there be such sorrowful contention? You honour what we honour, both alike: then we are brothers as concerns religion.

No decrying of other sects, ... no depreciation (of others) without cause, but on the contrary, a rendering of honour to other sects for whatever cause honour is due. By so doing, both one's own sect will be helped forward, and other sects benefited; by acting otherwise, one's own sect will be destroyed in injuring others.
But if others walk not righteously, we ought by righteous dealing to appease them: in this way, by showing its advantage, we cause religion everywhere to take deep hold and abide.
Who is a (true) spiritual teacher? He who, having grasped the essence of things, ever seeks to be of use to other beings.

Tell him...I look for no recompense—not even to be born in heaven—but seek ... the benefit of men, to bring back those who have gone astray, to enlighten those living in ... dismal error, ... to put away all sources of sorrow and pain from the world.
I CONSIDER the welfare of all people as something for which I must work.

Then the man ... said to himself: “ I will not keep all this treasure to myself; I will share it with others.” Upon this he went to king Brahmadatta, and said: ... “ Be it known to you I have discovered a treasure, and I wish it to be used for the good of the country.”
For the benefit, weal, and happiness of the people generally.
